name: B.S. in Mechanical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan reconciles to 120 credits with a standard math/science base (MATH 111/112/211/222, PHYS 111/121, CHEM 121/122), but the technical spine is overwhelmingly Industrial Engineering: IE 203, IE 224, IE 331, IE 334/335/339, IE 355, IE 439/440/445, IE 441, IE 459/461/466 and an IE 443/444 capstone, with only MECH 320 (Statics & Strength), MECH 236 (Dynamics), ME 339 (Mechanical Design) and ECE 405 representing mechanical content. For a degree titled Mechanical Engineering the required plan omits the mechanical thermal-fluids core entirely, so the sequence does not progress toward ME competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the published plan of study with the degree title: restore the mechanical-engineering spine (thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, machine design sequence, vibrations/controls) in place of the industrial-engineering plan currently shown.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: deepestPrereqChain is 7 with well-formed upper-level chains (IE 331 depth 5; IE 440, IE 445, IE 459, IE 461 depth 6, each built on IE 331/MATH 112). All 5 dangling references (missingCourseCount 5: CHEM 125A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are chemistry/physics lab co-requisites and GER placeholders rather than real broken course links.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A lab and GER 200/300 catalog entries so the metrics show zero dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET-style educational objectives appear in the provided data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. For a flagship engineering B.S. this absence is a genuine catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / ABET student outcomes (design, analysis, experimentation, professional responsibility).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 (creditReconcileDelta 0.0), with terms loaded at 14-17 credits and a transparent split across GER, a math/science block, and the major core.
dimensions.suggestion: Trim the heavy 17-credit second-year second semester and add an explicit credit-summary table (major core vs. math/science vs. GER vs. technical electives).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and legible, but the page misrepresents the program: a Mechanical Engineering plan is populated with the Industrial Engineering curriculum. It also carries parsing artifacts (a null-code, 0-credit 'FYS SEM' row and 'Select one of the following:' rows with null credits) and a course-title typo ('Idustrial Simulation', IE 445).
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the plan-of-study content to match the ME degree and fix the null-code rows and the 'Idustrial Simulation' typo.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As published, the required plan cannot demonstrate current mechanical-engineering practice because the mechanical core is absent: there is no thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, CAD/CAM, FEA, or mechatronics requirement. The IE content that is present (IE 445 simulation, IE 461 quality, IE 459 supply chain) is reasonably current for industrial engineering but does not address ME currency.
dimensions.suggestion: After restoring the ME spine, add modern mechanical content (FEA/CAE, mechatronics/robotics, additive manufacturing) so the plan reflects contemporary ME practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific with real technical content (IE 440 on Markov chains/queueing/inventory, IE 445 on discrete-event simulation, IE 461 on process capability and defect prevention, MECH/ME entries on statics, dynamics and design); only a few GER/humanities entries are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with sensible grade and standing conditions (e.g., IE 440 'IE 331 and MATH 112', IE 331 'MATH 211', IE 461 'IE 331'); minor ambiguity appears in 'IE 331 or equivalent' (IE 445) and the 5 dangling lab/GER placeholders.

missingTopics.rationale: A mechanical-engineering B.S. requires thermodynamics and heat transfer, yet neither appears in the required plan (only MECH 320/236 and ME 339 are mechanical); the department's MET 303/MET 404 equivalents show the content exists and should anchor the ME core.
Fluid mechanics is foundational to ME (turbomachinery, HVAC, aerodynamics) but is absent from the shown plan; add a required fluid-mechanics course to the sophomore/junior sequence.
The plan has no vibrations or dynamic-systems/controls course; modern mechanical systems demand both, so a vibrations and a controls course should join MECH 236 Dynamics.
Design is represented only by ME 339; a FEA/CAE course (the department offers ME 425 Finite Element Method) is expected in a contemporary ME curriculum for stress and thermal simulation.

missingTopics.topic: CAD/CAM and manufacturing processes for mechanical design
missingTopics.rationale: Only IE 203 computer graphics and IE 224 process design appear; ME graduates need parametric CAD, GD&T and CAM tied to mechanical design.

emergingTrends.trend: Additive manufacturing and design for AM
emergingTrends.rationale: 3D printing is reshaping mechanical prototyping and production; the department's CHE 415 Introduction to 3D Printing could be adopted as an ME elective.
Simulation-driven design and digital twins are now standard in mechanical product development; the design and dynamics courses (ME 339, MECH 236) are natural hosts.
Mechanical systems increasingly integrate sensing, actuation and control; a mechatronics/robotics requirement (cf. ME 431 Robotics and Automation) would modernize the plan.
Electrification, renewables and energy efficiency are central to ME hiring; a thermal-systems/energy course should build on a restored thermodynamics core.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for engineering design and analysis
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven modeling and ML surrogates are entering mechanical analysis workflows; the university's ENGR 301 Engineering Applications of Data Science could serve as a bridge elective.

topSuggestions: Reconcile the plan of study with the degree title: the published B.S. Mechanical Engineering plan is populated with the Industrial Engineering curriculum (IE 203/224/331/.../466 with an IE 443/444 capstone) and omits the mechanical thermal-fluids core.
Restore/require the mechanical core: thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, a machine-design sequence, and vibrations/controls.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes / ABET student outcomes, which are absent from the data.
Add modern mechanical content (FEA/CAE via ME 425, mechatronics/robotics via ME 431, additive manufacturing via CHE 415).
Resolve the 5 dangling references (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A labs, GER 200/300) and fix the 'Idustrial Simulation' (IE 445) title typo.
